From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Agreement between geographical entities

"Sister cities", "Twin town", and "Partner city" redirect here. For the films, see [*Sister Cities* (film)](/wiki/Sister_Cities_(film) "Sister Cities (film)") and [*Twin Town*](/wiki/Twin_Town "Twin Town"). For the phenomenon of cities located close to each other, see [Twin cities](/wiki/Twin_cities "Twin cities"). For the album by the Wonder Years, see [Sister Cities (album)](/wiki/Sister_Cities_(album) "Sister Cities (album)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Twin_town_sign_Oskarshamn_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Twin_town_sign_Oskarshamn_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Twin_town_sign_Oskarshamn_(cropped).jpg)

[Fingerposts](/wiki/Fingerpost "Fingerpost") in [Oskarshamn, Sweden](/wiki/Oskarshamn "Oskarshamn"), showing the direction from the post to each of the city's twin towns

A **sister city** or a **twin town** relationship is [a form of legal or social agreement](/wiki/International_relations "International relations") between two geographically and politically distinct localities for the purpose of promoting cultural and commercial ties.[[1]](#cite_note-Clarke-1) While there are early examples of international links between municipalities akin to what are now known as sister cities dating back to the 9th century,[[2]](#cite_note-Origins-2) the modern concept was first established and adopted worldwide during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4)

## Origins of the modern concept

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sister_city&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Origins of the modern concept")]

Throughout history, many cities have participated in various cultural exchanges and similar activities that might resemble a sister-city or twin-city relationship, but the first officially documented case of such a relationship was a signed agreement between the leaders of the cities of [Toledo, United States](/wiki/Toledo,_Ohio "Toledo, Ohio") and [Toledo, Spain](/wiki/Toledo,_Spain "Toledo, Spain") in 1931.[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5) However, the modern concept of town twinning appeared during the Second World War. More specifically, it was inspired by the bombing of [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry") on 14 November 1940, known as the [Coventry Blitz](/wiki/Coventry_Blitz "Coventry Blitz").[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) First conceived by the then [Mayor of Coventry](/wiki/Mayor_of_Coventry "Mayor of Coventry"), [Alfred Robert Grindlay](/wiki/Alfred_Robert_Grindlay "Alfred Robert Grindlay"),[[6]](#cite_note-:02-6) culminating in a telegram to the people of [Stalingrad](/wiki/Stalingrad "Stalingrad") (now [Volgograd](/wiki/Volgograd "Volgograd")) in 1942,[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) the idea emerged as a way of establishing solidarity links between cities in allied countries that went through similar devastating events.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The comradeship between the two cities continued, when again in response to the [Battle of Stalingrad](/wiki/Battle_of_Stalingrad "Battle of Stalingrad"), 830 women in Coventry – led by the subsequent Mayor [Emily Smith](/wiki/Emily_Smith_(mayor) "Emily Smith (mayor)") – had their names embroidered on a tablecloth along with the words "Little help is better than a lot of pity" and sent it, along with money (each donated six pence), to the people of Stalingrad.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4)[[12]](#cite_note-12) The tablecloth can now be seen at the Panorama Museum of the Battle of Stalingrad. The twinning between Coventry and Stalingrad was formalized in 1944[[13]](#cite_note-13) and, after the end of the war, similar links were established to foster friendship and understanding among former foes as an act of peace and reconciliation,[[2]](#cite_note-Origins-2)[[14]](#cite_note-Limousin-14) with new twinnings between Coventry and German cities: [Kiel](/wiki/Kiel "Kiel") as early as in 1947 and [Dresden](/wiki/Dresden "Dresden") in 1956.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) In 1957, Coventry was officially twinned with Belgrade, even though the link actually dates back to 1953 when then Yugoslav Ambassador visited Coventry and offered a gift of timber from his native country for use in the new Civic Theatre, which when finished was named [Belgrade Theatre](/wiki/Belgrade_Theatre "Belgrade Theatre").[[15]](#cite_note-15) The purpose of twinnings was then expanded to encourage trade and tourism.[[1]](#cite_note-Clarke-1) or to reflect other links, such as towns sharing the same name or migration links.[[16]](#cite_note-16) By the 2000s, town twinning may include localities of any scope such as villages, prefectures, or countries, and became increasingly used to form strategic international business links among member cities.[[17]](#cite_note-BBC-17)[[18]](#cite_note-Partners-18)

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sister_city&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Terminology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Gemellaggio_Castellabate-Blieskastel_%28cropped%29.JPG/250px-Gemellaggio_Castellabate-Blieskastel_%28cropped%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Gemellaggio_Castellabate-Blieskastel_(cropped).JPG)

A sample twinning agreement (between [Castellabate](/wiki/Castellabate "Castellabate"), Italy (left) and [Blieskastel](/wiki/Blieskastel "Blieskastel"), Germany (right))

In the United Kingdom, the term "twin towns" is most commonly used; the term "sister cities" is generally used for agreements with towns and cities in the Americas.[[1]](#cite_note-Clarke-1)[[19]](#cite_note-EU_Commission-19) In mainland Europe, the most commonly used terms are "twin towns", "partnership towns", "partner towns", and "friendship towns". The [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission") uses the term "twinned towns" and refers to the process as "town twinning".[[1]](#cite_note-Clarke-1)[[19]](#cite_note-EU_Commission-19) Spain uses the term "ciudades hermanadas", which means "sister cities". Germany, Poland, and the Czech Republic each use *Partnerstadt* (German), *miasto partnerskie* (Polish) and *partnerské město* (Czech), which translate as "partner town" or "partner city". France uses *ville jumelée* (jumelage, twinned town or city), and Italy has *gemellaggio* (twinning) and *comune gemellato* (twinned municipality).[[20]](#cite_note-20) In the Netherlands, the term is *jumelage*, *partnerstad* or *stedenband* ("city bond" when providing mutual support). In Greece, the word αδελφοποίηση (*adelphopiisi* – fraternisation) has been adopted. In Iceland, the terms *vinabæir* (friend towns) and *vinaborgir* (friend cities) are used. In the former [Soviet Bloc](/wiki/Eastern_Bloc "Eastern Bloc"), "twin towns" and "twin cities" were used;[[21]](#cite_note-Mogilev-21) in Russian, they use города-побратимы (sworn brother cities).[[22]](#cite_note-Trend-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The Americas, South Asia, and Australasia use the term "sister cities" or "twin cities". In China, the term is 友好城市 (yǒuhǎo chéngshì – friendly cities).[[24]](#cite_note-24) Sometimes, other government bodies enter into a twinning relationship, such as the agreement between the provinces of [Hainan](/wiki/Hainan "Hainan") in China and [Jeju](/wiki/Jeju_Province "Jeju Province") in South Korea. The [Douzelage](/wiki/Douzelage "Douzelage") is a town twinning association with one town from each of the member states of the European Union.[[1]](#cite_note-Clarke-1)[[25]](#cite_note-25) Although the term is often used interchangeably with the term "friendship city", this may mean a relationship with a more limited scope in comparison to a sister city relationship, and friendship city relationships are mayor-to-mayor agreements.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### City diplomacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sister_city&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: City diplomacy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/George_M._Sullivan_meets_the_mayor_of_Troms%C3%B8%2C_1969_%28Enhanced%29.png/250px-George_M._Sullivan_meets_the_mayor_of_Troms%C3%B8%2C_1969_%28Enhanced%29.png)](/wiki/File:George_M._Sullivan_meets_the_mayor_of_Troms%C3%B8,_1969_(Enhanced).png)

From left, mayors [Kåre Nordgård](/w/index.php?title=K%C3%A5re_Nordg%C3%A5rd&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kåre Nordgård (page does not exist)") of [Tromsø](/wiki/Troms%C3%B8 "Tromsø") and [George M. Sullivan](/wiki/George_M._Sullivan "George M. Sullivan") of [Anchorage, Alaska](/wiki/Anchorage,_Alaska "Anchorage, Alaska") meet in September 1969 to kick off their sister city relationship.

City diplomacy is a form of [paradiplomacy](/wiki/Paradiplomacy "Paradiplomacy") that involves discussions between officials and residents of different cities. Often these cities will be located in different countries. As such, city diplomacy involves a sort of [international relations](/wiki/International_relations "International relations") that works in parallel to the conventional system involving embassies, ambassadors, and treaties negotiated at the level of [nation states](/wiki/Nation_state "Nation state"). According to [Rodrigo Tavares](/wiki/Rodrigo_Tavares "Rodrigo Tavares"), the earliest formal attempts to establish city diplomacy across national boundaries took place in the 19th century. Only a handful of cities were involved in the 19th-century efforts; it was not until the turn of the millennium that it became much more common.[[27]](#cite_note-global_players-27) The first priority of those carrying out city diplomacy typically overlaps with the core aims of municipal government – improving the lives of local residents. Yet they will often collaborate with peers in other cities to work on issues of planet-wide concern, such as efforts to [address climate change](/wiki/Climate_change_mitigation "Climate change mitigation").[[28]](#cite_note-Future_of_Diplomacy-28)[[27]](#cite_note-global_players-27)

The phrase "city diplomacy" is formally used in the workings of the [United Cities and Local Governments](/wiki/United_Cities_and_Local_Governments "United Cities and Local Governments") and the [C40 Cities Climate Leadership Group](/wiki/C40_Cities_Climate_Leadership_Group "C40 Cities Climate Leadership Group"), and is recognised by the [USC Center on Public Diplomacy](/wiki/USC_Center_on_Public_Diplomacy "USC Center on Public Diplomacy"). A March 2014 debate in the British [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") acknowledged the evolution of town twinning into city diplomacy, particularly in matters of trade and tourism, but also in culture and post-conflict reconciliation.[[29]](#cite_note-29) The importance of cities developing "their own foreign economic policies on trade, foreign investment, tourism and attracting foreign talent" has also been highlighted by the [World Economic Forum](/wiki/World_Economic_Forum "World Economic Forum").[[30]](#cite_note-30) In addition to C40, other organisations facilitating city diplomacy include the [World Cities Summit](/wiki/World_Cities_Summit "World Cities Summit"), [City Mayors Foundation](/wiki/City_Mayors_Foundation "City Mayors Foundation"), the *Smart City Expo World Congress*,[[31]](#cite_note-31) the *Strong City Network*[[32]](#cite_note-32) and *100 Resilient Cities.*[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[28]](#cite_note-Future_of_Diplomacy-28) As of 2016, there were over 125 such multilateral networks and forums to facilitate international collaboration between different municipal authorities.[[27]](#cite_note-global_players-27) A Bill introduced in the 2019 session of the [US Congress](/wiki/US_Congress "US Congress") would have legislated for a City and State Diplomacy Act to create a new Office of Subnational Diplomacy at the [Department of State](/wiki/Department_of_State "Department of State") ( H.R.3571 - City and State Diplomacy Act]). In the 2020s, the field of city diplomacy has taken a step beyond city-to-city exchanges to target the facilitation of dialogue between cities and multilateral organs, such as the United Nations.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

### Twinning beyond cities and towns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sister_city&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Twinning beyond cities and towns")]

Not only cities and towns, but also provinces, states, territories, and regions enter into twinning agreements. For example, the [Canadian province of Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta") has "sister province" agreements with [Gangwon, South Korea](/wiki/Gangwon_Province,_South_Korea "Gangwon Province, South Korea") (since 1974), [Hokkaido, Japan](/wiki/Hokkaido "Hokkaido") (since 1980), [Heilongjiang, China](/wiki/Heilongjiang "Heilongjiang") (1981), [Jalisco, Mexico](/wiki/Jalisco "Jalisco") (1999), [Ivano-Frankivsk, Ukraine](/wiki/Ivano-Frankivsk_Oblast "Ivano-Frankivsk Oblast") (2004), [Lviv, Ukraine](/wiki/Lviv_Oblast "Lviv Oblast") (2005), [Guangdong, China](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong") (2017), and [California, United States](/wiki/California "California") (2018), as well as policy-oriented (as opposed to economic and cultural) agreements with [Nevada, United States](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada") (since 2013), [Missouri, United States](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri") (2015), and [Texas, United States](/wiki/Texas "Texas") (2018).[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)

## Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sister_city&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Europe")]

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of twin towns and sister cities in Europe](/wiki/List_of_twin_towns_and_sister_cities_in_Europe "List of twin towns and sister cities in Europe").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Castro_Pretorio_-_Colonna_di_Parigi_alle_Terme_di_Diocleziano_1010023.JPG/120px-Castro_Pretorio_-_Colonna_di_Parigi_alle_Terme_di_Diocleziano_1010023.JPG)](/wiki/File:Castro_Pretorio_-_Colonna_di_Parigi_alle_Terme_di_Diocleziano_1010023.JPG)

Column dedicated to Paris in Rome

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Jean-Yves_S%C3%A9nant_and_dignitaries_from_Antony%2C_France.jpg/250px-Jean-Yves_S%C3%A9nant_and_dignitaries_from_Antony%2C_France.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jean-Yves_S%C3%A9nant_and_dignitaries_from_Antony,_France.jpg)

Dignitaries from [Antony](/wiki/Antony,_Hauts-de-Seine "Antony, Hauts-de-Seine"), France attended a parade in twin city [Lexington, Massachusetts](/wiki/Lexington,_Massachusetts "Lexington, Massachusetts"), USA

The earliest known town twinning in Europe was between [Paderborn](/wiki/Paderborn "Paderborn"), Germany, and [Le Mans](/wiki/Le_Mans "Le Mans"), France, in 836.[[2]](#cite_note-Origins-2)[[37]](#cite_note-Balavoine1994-37) Starting in 1905, [Keighley](/wiki/Keighley "Keighley") in West Yorkshire, England, had a twinning arrangement with French communities [Suresnes](/wiki/Suresnes "Suresnes") and [Puteaux](/wiki/Puteaux "Puteaux").[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-France_Magazine_-_Twin_Towns-39) The first recorded modern twinning agreement was between Keighley and [Poix-du-Nord](/wiki/Poix-du-Nord "Poix-du-Nord") in Nord, France, in 1920 following the end of the [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I").[[18]](#cite_note-Partners-18)[[39]](#cite_note-France_Magazine_-_Twin_Towns-39)[[40]](#cite_note-lga-40)[[41]](#cite_note-East_Street-41) This was initially referred to as an adoption of the French town; formal twinning charters were not exchanged until 1986.[[42]](#cite_note-telegraph&argus-42)

The practice was continued after the Second World War as a way to promote mutual understanding and cross-border projects of mutual benefit.[[1]](#cite_note-Clarke-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Origins-2)[[43]](#cite_note-Twinning1-43)[[44]](#cite_note-Twin_Towns-44)[[45]](#cite_note-Regional_Dev-45) For example, [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry") twinned with [Stalingrad](/wiki/Volgograd "Volgograd") and later with [Dresden](/wiki/Dresden "Dresden") as an act of peace and reconciliation, all three cities having been heavily bombed during the war.[[1]](#cite_note-Clarke-1)[[41]](#cite_note-East_Street-41)[[46]](#cite_note-Coventry_twinnings-46)[[47]](#cite_note-Coventry_twins-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) The city of [Bath](/wiki/Bath,_Somerset "Bath, Somerset") formed an "[Alkmaar](/wiki/Alkmaar "Alkmaar") Adoption committee" in March 1945, when the Dutch city was still occupied by the German Army in the final months of the war, and children from each city took part in exchanges in 1945 and 1946.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Similarly, in 1947, [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol "Bristol") Corporation (later Bristol City Council) sent five "leading citizens" on a goodwill mission to [Hanover](/wiki/Hanover "Hanover").[[18]](#cite_note-Partners-18)[[41]](#cite_note-East_Street-41) [Reading](/wiki/Reading,_Berkshire "Reading, Berkshire") in 1947 was the first British town to form links with a former "enemy" city – [Düsseldorf](/wiki/D%C3%BCsseldorf "Düsseldorf"), a link that still exists.[[50]](#cite_note-50) Since 9 April 1956 Rome and Paris have been exclusively and reciprocally twinned with each other, following the motto: "Only Paris is worthy of Rome; only Rome is worthy of Paris."[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-Paris1-52)

Within Europe, town twinning is supported by the European Union.[[1]](#cite_note-Clarke-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Origins-2)[[18]](#cite_note-Partners-18) The support scheme was established in 1989. In 2003 an annual budget of about [€](/wiki/Euro "Euro")12 million was allocated to about 1,300 projects. The [Council of European Municipalities and Regions](/wiki/Council_of_European_Municipalities_and_Regions "Council of European Municipalities and Regions") also works closely with the commission (DG Education and Culture) to promote modern, high quality twinning initiatives and exchanges that involve all sections of the community. It has launched a website dedicated to town twinning.[[53]](#cite_note-53) As of 1995, the European Union had more than 7,000 bilateral relationships involving almost 10,000 European municipalities, primarily French (2837 twinnings) and German (2485 twinnings).[[45]](#cite_note-Regional_Dev-45)

The painting of Minden

[Public art](/wiki/Public_art "Public art") has been used to celebrate twin town links, for instance in the form of seven mural paintings in the centre of the town of [Sutton](/wiki/Sutton,_London "Sutton, London"), Greater London. The five main paintings show a number of the main features of the [London Borough of Sutton](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Sutton "London Borough of Sutton") and its four twin towns, along with the [heraldic shield](/wiki/Escutcheon_(heraldry) "Escutcheon (heraldry)") of each above the other images. Each painting also features a plant as a visual representation of its town's [environmental awareness](/wiki/Environmentalism "Environmentalism").[[54]](#cite_note-54) In the case of Sutton this is in a separate smaller painting (above its main one) showing a beech tree, intended as a symbol of prosperity and from which [Carshalton Beeches](/wiki/Carshalton "Carshalton") in the borough derives its name.[[55]](#cite_note-Press_release-55) Another example of the use of public art is the wall sculpture of the partner cities of [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"), Germany.

A recent study has concluded that geographical distance has very little, if any, influence upon communities' selections of a twin town.[[56]](#cite_note-Arxiv-56) Twinned towns are often chosen because of similarities between them; thus about 15 towns in Wales are twinned with towns in [Brittany](/wiki/Brittany "Brittany"), and [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford "Oxford") is with [Bonn](/wiki/Bonn "Bonn"), [Leiden](/wiki/Leiden "Leiden"), [Grenoble](/wiki/Grenoble "Grenoble") and other university cities.[[1]](#cite_note-Clarke-1) In Italy, sets of twins are [Rovigo](/wiki/Rovigo "Rovigo") with [Viernheim](/wiki/Viernheim "Viernheim"), [Bedford](/wiki/Bedford "Bedford") and [Tulcea](/wiki/Tulcea "Tulcea"). Many former [West German](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") cities are twinned with former East German cities; these twinning links were established before the fall of the [Iron Curtain](/wiki/Iron_Curtain "Iron Curtain"). Famous examples are the partnerships of Hanover and [Leipzig](/wiki/Leipzig "Leipzig"), both of which have important [trade fair grounds](/wiki/Trade_fair "Trade fair"), or between [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg") and Dresden. The first US-German town twinning was in 1947 between [Worthington, Minnesota](/wiki/Worthington,_Minnesota "Worthington, Minnesota") and [Crailsheim](/wiki/Crailsheim "Crailsheim").[[1]](#cite_note-Clarke-1)
[St Petersburg](/wiki/St_Petersburg#International_relations "St Petersburg") in Russia holds the record for the largest number of partnership arrangements with other communities.[[56]](#cite_note-Arxiv-56) In June 2012, the Scottish village of [Dull](/wiki/Dull,_Perth_and_Kinross "Dull, Perth and Kinross") and the US town of [Boring, Oregon](/wiki/Boring,_Oregon "Boring, Oregon"), agreed to twin their municipalities to promote tourism in both places, playing on their names.[[57]](#cite_note-Dull_and_Boring-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59)

In the early 21st century, some towns made novelty twinning arrangements with fictional or virtual locations. For example, [Wincanton](/wiki/Wincanton "Wincanton"), England is partnered with [Ankh-Morpork](/wiki/Ankh-Morpork "Ankh-Morpork") from [Terry Pratchett](/wiki/Terry_Pratchett "Terry Pratchett")'s [Discworld books](/wiki/Discworld "Discworld"),[[60]](#cite_note-BBC/Pratchett-60) and the Isle of Skye, Scotland is twinned with the virtual [Skylands](/wiki/Skylanders "Skylanders").[[61]](#cite_note-61) Town twinning has increasingly been used to form strategic international business links. For example, in the 1990s, when the [Nottingham City Council](/wiki/Nottingham_City_Council "Nottingham City Council") in the UK considered installing a tram network, it consulted experts from its twin city of [Karlsruhe](/wiki/Karlsruhe "Karlsruhe"), which has one of the most extensive and efficient tram networks in Germany. With assistance from Karlsruhe's specialist engineers, Nottingham completed its second tram line in 2013.[[17]](#cite_note-BBC-17) In 2014, [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol "Bristol") and [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") announced their intention to form a "tuning" partnership based on a shared musical heritage and culture offer, at the initiative of Bristol Mayor [George Ferguson](/wiki/George_Ferguson_(architect) "George Ferguson (architect)").[[62]](#cite_note-Post/Ferguson-62) [Annecy](/wiki/Annecy "Annecy"), France, and [Nerima](/wiki/Nerima "Nerima"), Tokyo have for several years shared a partnership based on their "co-existent animation industry".[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64)

[Thessaloniki](/wiki/Thessaloniki "Thessaloniki") [stele](/wiki/Stele "Stele"), in sister city [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne")[[65]](#cite_note-Greek_twinnings-65)

[Toledo, United States](/wiki/Toledo,_Ohio "Toledo, Ohio") twinned with [Toledo, Spain](/wiki/Toledo,_Spain "Toledo, Spain") in 1931, and was the first city in North America to engage in town twinning.[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5) [Vancouver, Canada](/wiki/Vancouver,_British_Columbia "Vancouver, British Columbia") twinned[[66]](#cite_note-66) with [Odesa, Ukraine](/wiki/Odesa,_Ukraine "Odesa, Ukraine") in 1944, was the first twinned city in Canada and the second in North America, while [Denver, Colorado](/wiki/Denver,_Colorado "Denver, Colorado"), twinned with [Brest, France](/wiki/Brest,_France "Brest, France"), was the second twinned city in the United States. [Liberal, Kansas](/wiki/Liberal,_Kansas "Liberal, Kansas") was twinned with [Olney, United Kingdom](/wiki/Olney,_Buckinghamshire "Olney, Buckinghamshire") in 1950, and the cities have run a joint Pancake Day race ever since.[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-Olney_race-68) Littleton, Colorado, twinned with Bega, Australia, in 1961. [Tashkent](/wiki/Tashkent "Tashkent"), the capital city of Uzbekistan, was twinned with [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle"), Washington, in 1973. [Rochester, Minnesota](/wiki/Rochester,_Minnesota "Rochester, Minnesota"), and [Knebworth](/wiki/Knebworth "Knebworth"), UK, both centers for primary medical research, twinned in 1967. [Ontario, California](/wiki/Ontario,_California "Ontario, California"), has five sister cities around the world. They are Brockville, Ontario, Canada (since 1977); Guamúchil, Sinaloa, Mexico (since 1982); Mocorito, Sinaloa, Mexico (since 1982); Los Mochis, Sinaloa, Mexico (since 1988); and Winterthur, Canton of Zürich, Switzerland. [Oakville, Ontario](/wiki/Oakville,_Ontario "Oakville, Ontario") is twinned with [Dorval, Quebec](/wiki/Dorval,_Quebec "Dorval, Quebec"); [Huai'an, China](/wiki/Huai%27an "Huai'an"), and [Neyagawa, Osaka](/wiki/Neyagawa,_Osaka "Neyagawa, Osaka"), Japan.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

Town twinning begins for a variety of reasons. Generally, partner towns have similar demographics and size. They may arise from business connections, travel, similar industries, diaspora communities, or shared history. For example, the partnership between [Portland, Oregon](/wiki/Portland,_Oregon "Portland, Oregon") and [Bologna](/wiki/Bologna "Bologna"), Italy arose from shared industries in [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology") and education, and a "similar attitude towards food",[[70]](#cite_note-70) whereas [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago")'s link with [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw"), Poland began with Chicago's historic [Polish community](/wiki/Poles_in_Chicago "Poles in Chicago").[[71]](#cite_note-71) The twinning of [Indianapolis](/wiki/Indianapolis "Indianapolis") with [Monza](/wiki/Monza "Monza"), Italy, is due to both cities' long association with [auto racing](/wiki/Auto_racing "Auto racing"). Or in the case of [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta") and [Tbilisi](/wiki/Tbilisi "Tbilisi"), the two cities twinned over their shared status as a capital of Georgia.

A twin towns program was instituted in the United States in 1956 when President [Dwight D. Eisenhower](/wiki/Dwight_D._Eisenhower "Dwight D. Eisenhower") proposed a citizen diplomacy initiative. [Sister Cities International](/wiki/Sister_Cities_International "Sister Cities International") (SCI) was originally a program of the [National League of Cities](/wiki/National_League_of_Cities "National League of Cities"), but it became a separate corporation in 1967 due to the growth and popularity of the program.[[72]](#cite_note-sister-cities.org-72) Twin town cultural events include the annual [National Cherry Blossom Festival](/wiki/National_Cherry_Blossom_Festival "National Cherry Blossom Festival") in Washington, D.C., honoring Washington's twin relationship with [Tokyo City](/wiki/Tokyo_City "Tokyo City"). Many twinned towns developed business agreements with their partners. For example, [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont")'s [Ben & Jerry's](/wiki/Ben_%26_Jerry%27s "Ben & Jerry's") Ice Cream company opened a factory in the [Republic of Karelia](/wiki/Republic_of_Karelia "Republic of Karelia") in Russia and offered the same profit-sharing plan to its Russian employees.

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of twin towns and sister cities in Asia](/wiki/List_of_twin_towns_and_sister_cities_in_Asia "List of twin towns and sister cities in Asia").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Isfahankualalumpur.jpg/250px-Isfahankualalumpur.jpg)](/wiki/File:Isfahankualalumpur.jpg)

Esfahan Street in [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur "Kuala Lumpur") (top) and Kuala Lumpur Avenue in [Isfahan](/wiki/Isfahan "Isfahan") (bottom)

China's sister city relationships are managed by the [Chinese People's Association for Friendship with Foreign Countries](/wiki/Chinese_People%27s_Association_for_Friendship_with_Foreign_Countries "Chinese People's Association for Friendship with Foreign Countries"), a [united front](/wiki/United_Front_(China) "United Front (China)") organization.[[73]](#cite_note-73) Sister city initiatives are an increasingly widespread mechanism for Chinese [public diplomacy](/wiki/Public_diplomacy "Public diplomacy").[[74]](#cite_note-:Curtis&Klaus-74) From the early 2000s until 2024, the number of China's sister city relationships doubled.[[74]](#cite_note-:Curtis&Klaus-74) More than one-third of Chinese sister city relationships are with sister cities in the east Asia Pacific region.[[74]](#cite_note-:Curtis&Klaus-74)

Town twinning is supported in Japan by the [Council of Local Authorities for International Relations](/wiki/Council_of_Local_Authorities_for_International_Relations "Council of Local Authorities for International Relations"), a joint agency of local governments established by the Japanese government in 1988 (similar to [Sister Cities International](/wiki/Sister_Cities_International "Sister Cities International"), its counterpart in the US). In Japan, the international city relations may be split into multiple terms, such as Sister Cities, Friendship Cooperation Cities, Business Partner Cities (BPC), Memorandum of Understanding (MOU), Sister Ports / Friendship Ports, etc.[[75]](#cite_note-75) China mostly uses the term "friendship cooperation cities" rather than "sister cities", as the Chinese words for sisters, "姐妹" (reading: jiěmèi, literally elder sister and younger sister), could imply a hierarchical relationship. In the 2010s, Tokyo began to actively promote 'city diplomacy' with other global cities at the initiative of governor [Yoichi Masuzoe](/wiki/Yoichi_Masuzoe "Yoichi Masuzoe").[[76]](#cite_note-76)

## Linguistic reasons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sister_city&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Linguistic reasons")]

Relationships between communities can also arise because of shared names; they may be named after one community (as in the case of [Córdoba](/wiki/C%C3%B3rdoba,_Andalusia#Twin_towns_–_sister_cities "Córdoba, Andalusia")), they may share names (as in the case of [Santiago de Compostela](/wiki/Santiago_de_Compostela#Twin_towns/Sister_cities "Santiago de Compostela")), or their names may have a common [etymology](/wiki/Etymology "Etymology"). These similarities usually arise from sharing the same or related language or having been a colony or previously conquered.

## Political significance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sister_city&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Political significance")]

The twinning of towns and cities is sometimes done for political purposes. The Hungarian city [Gyöngyös](/wiki/Gy%C3%B6ngy%C3%B6s "Gyöngyös") was twinned with the Azerbaijani city of [Shusha](/wiki/Shusha "Shusha") in 2013, signing the twinning agreement with representatives from the Azerbaijani government; Hungary recognised Shusha as *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* part of [Azerbaijan](/wiki/Azerbaijan "Azerbaijan"), even though it was controlled at the time and [until 2020](/wiki/Battle_of_Shusha_(2020) "Battle of Shusha (2020)") by the military forces of [Armenia](/wiki/Armenia "Armenia") and the unrecognised [Republic of Artsakh](/wiki/Republic_of_Artsakh "Republic of Artsakh").[[77]](#cite_note-77) An attempt was made in 2003 by [Preston](/wiki/Preston,_Lancashire "Preston, Lancashire") city councillors in England to twin with the Palestinian town of [Nablus](/wiki/Nablus "Nablus") in the name of solidarity.[[78]](#cite_note-78) [Turkey](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Turkey "Foreign relations of Turkey") bans partnerships with any city in a country that recognizes the [Armenian genocide](/wiki/Armenian_genocide "Armenian genocide"). As a result, when [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria") recognized the genocide in 2016, some twin agreements such as [Edirne](/wiki/Edirne "Edirne")–[Haskovo](/wiki/Haskovo "Haskovo") were terminated by Turkey.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

[China](/wiki/China "China") manages sister city relationships through the [Chinese People's Association for Friendship with Foreign Countries](/wiki/Chinese_People%27s_Association_for_Friendship_with_Foreign_Countries "Chinese People's Association for Friendship with Foreign Countries") (CPAFFC). In April 2019, CPAFFC president [Li Xiaolin](/wiki/Li_Xiaolin_(politician) "Li Xiaolin (politician)") said: "Friendship city relations have become one of the important channels to implement the [Belt and Road Initiative](/wiki/Belt_and_Road_Initiative "Belt and Road Initiative")."[[80]](#cite_note-80) In January 2020, [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai") canceled its sister city relationship with [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague") after Prague's mayor signed a sister city relationship with [Taipei](/wiki/Taipei "Taipei"). In November 2020, US senator [Marsha Blackburn](/wiki/Marsha_Blackburn "Marsha Blackburn") introduced legislation, the Sister City Transparency Act, to provide federal oversight to mitigate risks of sister city agreements being used for political influence campaigns.[[81]](#cite_note-81) In 2024, Indiana banned localities from entering into sister city agreements with six "foreign adversary" countries.[[82]](#cite_note-82)[[83]](#cite_note-83) In August 2025, Texas governor [Greg Abbott](/wiki/Greg_Abbott "Greg Abbott") signed a law prohibiting sister city agreements with countries designated foreign adversaries.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

## Termination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sister_city&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Termination")]

In 2011, several British towns ended their twinning arrangements.[[85]](#cite_note-85) In 2012, the city of [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing") suspended their sister city relationship with [Nagoya](/wiki/Nagoya "Nagoya") after [Nanjing Massacre denialist](/wiki/Nanjing_Massacre_denial "Nanjing Massacre denial") statements by Nagoya's mayor, [Takashi Kawamura](/wiki/Takashi_Kawamura_(politician) "Takashi Kawamura (politician)").[[86]](#cite_note-86) In 2013, the Italian cities of [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice"), and [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin"), formerly twinned with [Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg "Saint Petersburg"), suspended their links due to the [Russian anti-LGBTQ law](/wiki/Russian_anti-LGBTQ_law "Russian anti-LGBTQ law").[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88) Activists in [California](/wiki/California "California") circulated petitions urging California cities and counties with relationships with Russian counterparts to take similar steps.[[89]](#cite_note-Bay_Area_News-89) In 2014, [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague") terminated its partnership with Saint Petersburg and [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow") because of the [2014 Russian military intervention in Ukraine](/wiki/2014_Russian_military_intervention_in_Ukraine "2014 Russian military intervention in Ukraine"). Moscow had been Prague's partner city since 1995.[[90]](#cite_note-Prague_Post-90)

In 2017, [Hirofumi Yoshimura](/wiki/Hirofumi_Yoshimura "Hirofumi Yoshimura") (the mayor of [Osaka](/wiki/Osaka "Osaka")) ended the city's 60-year relationship with [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco") due to the erection of a [memorial](/wiki/Memorial "Memorial") (the [San Francisco Comfort Women Memorial](/wiki/San_Francisco_Comfort_Women_Memorial "San Francisco Comfort Women Memorial")) to [comfort women](/wiki/Comfort_women "Comfort women") in [downtown San Francisco](/wiki/Downtown_San_Francisco "Downtown San Francisco").[[91]](#cite_note-91)[[92]](#cite_note-92) Similarly, the cities of [Glendale, California](/wiki/Glendale,_California "Glendale, California"), and [Higashiōsaka](/wiki/Higashi%C5%8Dsaka "Higashiōsaka"), Japan, came close to terminating their twinning in 2013 and 2014 because of an ongoing dispute over Glendale's support for the erection of a statue dedicated to Korean comfort women in a city park.[[93]](#cite_note-93) In July 2020, the town council of [Nieuwegein](/wiki/Nieuwegein "Nieuwegein"), a Dutch city south of [Utrecht](/wiki/Utrecht "Utrecht"), voted to end its friendship with [Puławy](/wiki/Pu%C5%82awy "Puławy") in eastern Poland, citing "[gay free zones](/wiki/Gay_free_zones "Gay free zones")" as the reason.[[94]](#cite_note-94) In March 2017, [Gaziantep](/wiki/Gaziantep "Gaziantep") in Turkey unilaterally (but not formally) broke its *stedenband* with [Nijmegen](/wiki/Nijmegen "Nijmegen") in the Netherlands when the latter rejected pressure to side with the Turkish government amidst diplomatic tensions with the Dutch government (the so-called *Turkijerel*).[[95]](#cite_note-Nijmegen_Gaziantep-95) After several years of inactivity, in 2021 Nijmegen's city council formally terminated the *stedenband* formed with Gaziantep[[95]](#cite_note-Nijmegen_Gaziantep-95) in 2006, citing political and administrative reasons; yet when the February [2023 Turkey–Syria earthquakes](/wiki/2023_Turkey%E2%80%93Syria_earthquakes "2023 Turkey–Syria earthquakes") hit Gaziantep and its environs, the mayor of Nijmegen still sent a public letter of heartfelt sympathy and condolences to the mayor of Gaziantep.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

In March 2022, the [City of Melbourne](/wiki/City_of_Melbourne "City of Melbourne") council voted unanimously to suspend its relationship with Saint Petersburg as a result of the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine").[[97]](#cite_note-:2-97) In March 2022, [Doncaster](/wiki/Doncaster "Doncaster") cut tieswith their twin-town [Ozyorsk](/wiki/Ozyorsk,_Chelyabinsk_Oblast "Ozyorsk, Chelyabinsk Oblast"),[[98]](#cite_note-98) also due to the Russian invasion of Ukraine.[[97]](#cite_note-:2-97) On 22 March 2022, [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry") voted to temporarily pause the city's link with [Volgograd](/wiki/Volgograd "Volgograd") (formerly Stalingrad) and explore twinning with [Mariupol](/wiki/Mariupol "Mariupol") instead due to the Russian invasion of Ukraine.[[99]](#cite_note-99) On 28 March 2022, [Oak Ridge, Tennessee](/wiki/Oak_Ridge,_Tennessee "Oak Ridge, Tennessee"), temporarily suspended its relationship with [Obninsk](/wiki/Obninsk "Obninsk") due to the Russian invasion of Ukraine.[[100]](#cite_note-100) In July 2022, the city of [Eugene, Oregon](/wiki/Eugene,_Oregon "Eugene, Oregon"), suspended its ties to [Irkutsk](/wiki/Irkutsk "Irkutsk") due to the Russian invasion of Ukraine.[[101]](#cite_note-101) In February 2023, the mayor of [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona") broke ties with twin city [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel_Aviv "Tel Aviv"), citing claims of [Israeli apartheid](/wiki/Israeli_apartheid "Israeli apartheid") as the reason.[[102]](#cite_note-102)
